name: The 400 Huitznahua
shortDescription: The 400 Huitznahua were the sons of Coatlicue, slain by Huitzilopochtli upon his birth. They represent the stars of the southern sky and were associated with the night.
description: The 400 Huitznahua (also known as the Centzon Huitznahua) were the sons of the earth goddess Coatlicue. In Aztec mythology, they are best known for their role in the birth story of Huitzilopochtli, the god of war, sun, and sacrifice. Angered by their mother's miraculous impregnation by a ball of feathers, the Huitznahua, led by their sister Coyolxauhqui, plotted to kill Coatlicue. However, Huitzilopochtli sprang forth fully armed from his mother's womb and defeated them, scattering their bodies across the sky to become the stars of the southern constellation. They are often interpreted as representing the stars of the southern sky, and their defeat by Huitzilopochtli symbolizes the triumph of the sun over the darkness of night. Their association with the south and the night sky links them to themes of death, sacrifice, and the cyclical nature of time. While not inherently malevolent, their opposition to Huitzilopochtli positions them as antagonists within the myth, representing the forces that the sun god must overcome to bring forth the dawn. The number 400, while seemingly literal, is often interpreted as signifying 'innumerable' or 'countless' in Mesoamerican cultures.
appearance: Represented as stars, often depicted as warriors or constellations.
abilities: Associated with the night and southern sky

sources.title: Handbook to Life in the Ancient Aztec World
sources.author: Karl Taube

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Blood and Hearts
worshipPractices.description: While not directly worshipped, the Huitznahua's defeat by Huitzilopochtli served as a justification for human sacrifice. Victims, often war captives, were offered to Huitzilopochtli in a symbolic reenactment of his victory over his brothers, ensuring the sun's continued strength.

worshipPractices.name: Ritualistic Combat
worshipPractices.description: Certain ceremonies involved ritual combat, sometimes to the death, that mirrored the battle between Huitzilopochtli and the Huitznahua. These battles were intended to honor Huitzilopochtli and ensure the cyclical renewal of the cosmos.

festivals.name: Panquetzaliztli
festivals.timing: The 15th month of the Aztec calendar
festivals.description: This major festival celebrated Huitzilopochtli's birth and victory. Activities included processions, ritual battles reenacting Huitzilopochtli's defeat of the Centzon Huitznahua, and offerings to the god.

festivals.name: Toxcatl
festivals.timing: The 5th month of the Aztec calendar
festivals.description: While primarily dedicated to Tezcatlipoca, Toxcatl also included elements of honoring the ancestors and the cyclical nature of life and death. The defeat of the Huitznahua represented a critical point in this cycle.

cultural.modernLegacy: The story of the 400 Huitznahua continues to inspire artistic interpretations of Aztec mythology, particularly in visual arts and literature exploring themes of sibling rivalry, cosmic battles, and the origins of the Aztec pantheon. Their story also serves as a reminder of the Aztec emphasis on warfare and sacrifice in maintaining cosmic balance.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Like the Centzon Huitznahua, the Titans represent an older generation of deities who are challenged and overthrown by a younger, more powerful god (Zeus/Huitzilopochtli). Both groups display jealousy and resistance to the changing order.

The Jötnar are a race often in conflict with the gods (Aesir), similar to the Huitznahua's opposition to Huitzilopochtli. Both groups embody primal, chaotic forces that threaten the established divine order.

The children of Nut, initially prevented from being born on any day of the year, faced hardship and opposition, reflecting the Huitznahua's eventual defeat. Both narratives involve divine siblings and a struggle against predetermined fate or divine will.

The Asuras are often depicted as rivals to the Devas (gods), much like the Huitznahua oppose Huitzilopochtli. Both represent forces that challenge the established divine power, leading to conflict and the assertion of the dominant deity's authority.

associations.name: Four Hundred
associations.significance: Represents countlessness and a large multitude, emphasizing their vast numbers and collective power.

associations.name: Stars
associations.significance: They are associated with the stars of the southern sky, particularly the Pleiades, representing their celestial nature and connection to the night.

associations.name: Dark Blue
associations.significance: Represents the night sky and the southern direction, linking them to their celestial domain.

associations.name: Rebellion
associations.significance: Their plot to kill their mother symbolizes rebellion against the natural order and divine will.

associations.name: Obsidian
associations.significance: Relates to sacrifice and warfare due to the use of obsidian in weaponry, linking to their role in the Huitzilopochtli myth.

associations.name: Frustration
associations.significance: They represent the frustration of the old order with the advent of a new, powerful force.

associations.name: Serpent
associations.significance: Associated with the earth goddess Coatlicue, their mother, symbolizing their terrestrial origins and connection to the earth's power.

companions.name: Coatlicue
companions.description: Coatlicue is their mother. She is a powerful earth goddess who, despite their anger, ultimately births Huitzilopochtli, who defeats them.

companions.name: Coyolxauhqui
companions.description: Coyolxauhqui is their sister and leader. She incites them to kill their mother, Coatlicue.

companions.name: Huitzilopochtli
companions.description: Huitzilopochtli is their brother and nemesis. He is born fully armed and defeats them to protect his mother.

extendedContent.title: Origin and Nature: Stars of the South
extendedContent.content:
The 400 Huitznahua, more accurately translated as the 'Four Hundred Southerners,' are celestial beings in Aztec cosmology. Their very name, 'Centzon Huitznahua,' indicates a vast, uncountable multitude emanating from the southern direction, associated with the realm of the dead and the ancestral past. Born from Coatlicue, the earth goddess, they represent the stars of the southern sky, embodying celestial power and a link to the primordial forces of creation. Sahagún's Florentine Codex provides significant details about their parentage and association with Coatlicue, emphasizing their role as offspring of a fundamental deity. 

Their nature is inherently linked to their role in the celestial drama. As stars, they possess a certain level of agency and power, yet they are also subject to the cosmic order. Their rebellion against their mother, Coatlicue, and their subsequent defeat by Huitzilopochtli underscores the cyclical nature of Aztec mythology, where destruction and renewal are intertwined. The number '400' itself is symbolic, not necessarily literal. It signifies innumerability and vastness, highlighting the Huitznahua's collective power and their connection to the endless expanse of the night sky.

Scholarly interpretations often focus on the Huitznahua as embodiments of chaos and the forces that threaten cosmic order. Their attempted matricide represents a disruption of the established divine hierarchy and a challenge to the emerging power of Huitzilopochtli, the sun god. Therefore, their defeat is crucial for the establishment of the solar age and the triumph of order over chaos. Their origin from Coatlicue, the fertile earth, further emphasizes their inherent connection to the natural world and its cyclical processes of birth, death, and rebirth.

extendedContent.title: Encounters in Myth: The Birth of Huitzilopochtli
extendedContent.content:
The primary narrative involving the 400 Huitznahua revolves around the miraculous birth of Huitzilopochtli. Coatlicue, while sweeping the temple, was impregnated by a ball of feathers that fell from the sky. This immaculate conception enraged her numerous children, the Centzon Huitznahua, who viewed it as a dishonor and a threat to their established position. Led by their sister Coyolxauhqui, they plotted to kill their mother, intending to restore what they perceived as the proper order.

According to the accounts preserved in the Florentine Codex and other sources, the Huitznahua armed themselves and marched towards Coatlicue's location on Coatepec (Snake Mountain). Their intent was clear: to eliminate the perceived shame brought upon their family. However, before they could carry out their plan, Huitzilopochtli burst forth from his mother's womb, fully armed and ready for battle. This sudden emergence marked a pivotal moment in Aztec mythology, signifying the triumph of the sun god over the forces of darkness and chaos.

Huitzilopochtli, wielding the Xiuhcoatl (fire serpent), swiftly defeated his siblings, scattering their bodies across the mountain. Coyolxauhqui was decapitated, and her body tumbled down the slopes, symbolizing the fragmentation and defeat of the lunar forces. The remaining Huitznahua were vanquished, representing the subjugation of the stars by the rising sun. This event established Huitzilopochtli as the dominant deity and ushered in the solar era, a period of order and sacrifice maintained through continuous warfare and cosmic balance.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism and Meaning: Cosmic Conflict
extendedContent.content:
The 400 Huitznahua are powerful symbols within Aztec cosmology, representing the forces of darkness, the stars, and the potential for chaos that constantly threatens the established order. Their rebellion against Coatlicue and their subsequent defeat by Huitzilopochtli encapsulate the perpetual conflict between opposing forces that is central to Aztec worldview. The stars, while beautiful and seemingly constant, can be seen as a threat to the sun's dominance, their light fading with its rising. The Huitznahua embody this inherent tension.

The number '400' in their name is significant. In Mesoamerican cultures, large numbers often represent innumerability and completeness rather than a precise count. Thus, the 'Four Hundred Southerners' represent the entirety of the southern stars, a vast and powerful force. Their association with the south, the direction of the dead, further emphasizes their connection to the ancestral past and the primordial forces that precede the current solar age. Their attempted matricide can be interpreted as a symbolic rejection of the earth and its fertility, a challenge to the natural order that Huitzilopochtli ultimately defends.

Moreover, the Huitznahua’s defeat symbolizes the necessity of sacrifice to maintain cosmic balance. Huitzilopochtli's victory, achieved through violence and the scattering of their bodies, underscores the Aztec belief that the sun's continued existence requires constant nourishment and the subjugation of opposing forces. This narrative justifies the practice of human sacrifice, which was seen as a vital component in sustaining the sun's energy and ensuring the continuation of the world. The Huitznahua, therefore, become symbols of the necessary sacrifices for the maintenance of cosmic order.

extendedContent.title: Cultural Significance: Justification for Rituals
extendedContent.content:
The myth of the 400 Huitznahua and the birth of Huitzilopochtli held profound cultural significance for the Aztecs, shaping their religious beliefs, social structures, and ritual practices. The story served as a justification for warfare and human sacrifice, both of which were central to Aztec society. The Huitznahua's defeat by Huitzilopochtli provided a divine precedent for the subjugation of enemies and the offering of human hearts to nourish the sun and maintain cosmic balance. The Templo Mayor in Tenochtitlan, the Aztec capital, was built in the image of Coatepec, the mountain where the battle took place, further solidifying the connection between myth and reality.

The annual Panquetzaliztli festival celebrated the birth of Huitzilopochtli and commemorated his victory over the Huitznahua. During this festival, captives were sacrificed to represent the vanquished stars, reenacting the mythical battle and ensuring the sun's continued dominance. This ritualistic reenactment reinforced the Aztec worldview and instilled a sense of duty and obligation to the gods.

Furthermore, the myth of the Huitznahua served to legitimize the Aztec empire's expansion and its control over conquered territories. The subjugation of neighboring tribes was often framed as a continuation of the cosmic battle between Huitzilopochtli and the forces of chaos, with the Aztecs cast as the champions of order and the conquered peoples as representatives of the defeated Huitznahua. This narrative provided a powerful ideological justification for Aztec imperialism and the extraction of tribute from conquered regions.

subtitle: Aztec star gods who plotted against their mother.
